key_insight: Most “new” AI-generated inorganic crystals are not truly novel: they are usually duplicates of training structures or simple elemental substitutions of known prototypes.
engineering_relevance: For materials discovery workflows, this means generative models should be benchmarked against substitution-based baselines before being trusted to expand the search space.
method: Built a workflow to classify generated crystals as training duplicates, substitution-derived, or unmatched.
Applied the workflow to representative generative models of inorganic crystals.
Compared structural fingerprints to distinguish memorization from interpolation across symmetry classes.
evidence_signal: Across representative models, 81–92% of chemically valid and metastable generated crystals were either training duplicates or substitution-derived structures, with especially strong bias in high-symmetry systems.
watch_item: A model that appears to produce diverse crystals may still be overfitting known prototypes, so novelty claims should be checked against both training data and substitution rules.
paper_title: Substitution-Based Analysis of Structural Novelty for Generative Models of Materials
